content:
I opened the app yesterday expecting the usual interface. Instead I got a new logo and a mild identity crisis.

6 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

If you use NotebookLM regularly, you probably noticed something different in the last day or two. The name is gone.
As of July 16, 2026, Google has officially renamed NotebookLM to Gemini Notebook, and if you’re anything like me, your first reaction was somewhere between “did I miss an email” and “wait, is this even the same tool.”
Short answer: yes, it’s the same tool. But there’s more to this than a cosmetic rebrand, and I think it’s worth actually walking through what changed, what didn’t, and what it signals about where this is heading — because a few of the details matter more than the name change itself.
If you’ve built any real workflow around this tool — for research, client work, or ongoing projects — I’ve put together a complete system for getting the most out of it.
What Actually Changed
The core facts are straightforward, even if the branding shift feels a little disorienting at first. Google renamed NotebookLM to Gemini Notebook to align it with the rest of its Gemini product family — the same reasoning that turned Bard into Gemini back in February 2024. This is Google doing what Google has done repeatedly: launch something under an experimental name, let it mature, then fold the branding into the main Gemini umbrella once it’s proven itself.
And this product has proven itself. Google says Gemini Notebook now has more than 30 million users and is in use at over 600,000 organizations — numbers that make this one of the more significant renames in Google’s recent history, not just a minor tidy-up.
Here’s what I think is genuinely useful to know if you’ve been using this tool and are worried something’s about to break: nothing you’ve built is going away. Same website — notebooklm.google.com still works. Same notebooks, same sources, same Audio Overviews, same Studio outputs. If you’ve been using browser extensions or third-party tools built around NotebookLM, those keep working too, since the underlying site hasn’t moved anywhere. This is a naming and branding change layered on top of the existing product, not a migration to something new that leaves your work behind.
The logo changes to match Google’s blue-and-purple Gemini gradient. That’s genuinely the most visually obvious change day to day.
The Part That’s More Than Cosmetic
Here’s where I think the story gets more interesting than just “Google renamed a thing again.”
Alongside the rebrand, Google added native code execution — the ability for the tool to write and run actual code inside a secure, sandboxed cloud environment tied to your notebook. This isn’t just a bigger version of the text-based synthesis NotebookLM already did well. It means the tool can now perform real calculations, parse and reconcile inconsistent data formats, and produce verified numeric output — the kind of task that used to require exporting your data into a separate analysis tool.
I’d describe this as the product moving from “reads and explains your documents” to “reads, explains, and can now actually compute things from your documents.” That’s a meaningfully bigger capability than a fresh coat of paint, even if it’s arriving at the same moment as the name change.
There’s also a longer-term integration story here that I think matters more than people are giving it credit for. Notebooks are already available inside the main Gemini app, with syncing between that and the standalone Gemini Notebook experience. And Google has said notebooks are coming to AI Mode in Google Search — meaning, eventually, your own uploaded research and notes could function as context for AI-assisted search results. That’s a meaningfully different vision than a standalone research tool you visit separately from everything else you do.
Whether you know it as NotebookLM or Gemini Notebook, the actual skill — getting genuinely useful, grounded answers instead of vague summaries — hasn’t changed with the name. Here’s the complete system I built around exactly that.
Who Actually Gets the New Features Right Now
I want to be upfront about this part, because it’s easy to read headlines about “code execution” and assume it’s available to everyone immediately. It isn’t, at least not yet.
The code execution capability is rolling out first to Google AI Ultra subscribers and Workspace business customers with the right access tier. Standard Pro tier access is expected in the coming weeks, according to reporting from TechCrunch, but there’s no confirmed date for free tier users. If you’re on the free plan, the rename applies to you today, but the headline new capability doesn’t — at least not yet.
This is a pattern I’ve noticed with a lot of Google’s recent AI rollouts: the announcement covers the full vision, but actual access trickles down by tier over weeks or months. Worth knowing before you restructure how you work around a feature you might not actually have yet.
Why I Think the Name Actually Makes Sense
I’ll admit my first instinct with any corporate rebrand is mild irritation — another name to relearn, another moment of “wait, is this the same product,” another round of confused search results for a while as the old and new names both float around. But I think this one has a reasonable justification behind it, beyond pure brand consolidation.
“NotebookLM” always had that slightly awkward “LM” — language model — sitting in the name, which never meant much to anyone outside people who already follow AI closely. It was a fine name for a product in its experimental phase, less fine for a tool now used by tens of millions of people who mostly don’t think in AI-industry jargon. “Gemini Notebook” is a clearer description of what it actually is: a notebook, powered by Gemini, that happens to be excellent at grounding its answers in your own material.
There’s also a practical continuity argument for Google here. Bard’s rename to Gemini was confusing in the moment, but two years later, nobody thinks of it as two separate products — it’s just “Gemini” now. I’d expect the same trajectory here: mild confusion for a few weeks while both names circulate, and then Gemini Notebook simply becomes the name, the same way NotebookLM eventually did after its own earlier life as Project Tailwind back at Google I/O 2023.
What I’d Actually Do About This
If you’re a regular user, I don’t think there’s much to actually do here beyond noting the name change and moving on. Your notebooks are intact. Your workflow doesn’t need to change. If you’ve bookmarked notebooklm.google.com, that link still works.
The one thing genuinely worth paying attention to is the code execution rollout, if you do any kind of data-heavy research or analysis work. That capability is worth testing the moment it reaches your account tier, because it changes what the tool can do for you from “explain and synthesize” to “explain, synthesize, and calculate” — a real expansion, not just a semantic one.
Beyond that, I’d treat this the way I’ve learned to treat most Google rebrands at this point: a signal about where the product is heading — deeper into the Gemini ecosystem, more integrated with Search, more capable with data — more than a reason to change anything about how you’re currently using it.
The name changed. What actually makes this tool useful — specific prompts, well-structured notebooks, and knowing how to ask the right questions of your own material — hasn’t. I’ve put together the complete system for that here, covering research, learning, writing, and business use cases in depth.
